aihumma-root.label: AIHUMMA Learning Outcomes
aihumma-root.description: Taxonomy of learning outcomes for the AIHUMMA program.

cluster-tl.label: Technical Literacy
cluster-tl.description: Cluster TL — Understanding how AI and ML systems are built, trained, and evaluated. Spans conceptual literacy through hands-on implementation.

tl-01.label: ML Model Types
tl-01.description: TL-01 — Explain how core ML model types (classification, regression, clustering, deep learning, generative models) work at a conceptual level.

tl-02.label: Technical Documentation Critique
tl-02.description: TL-02 — Interpret technical AI documentation (model cards, capability evaluations, technical specs) as arguments rather than facts.

tl-03.label: ML Workflow Implementation
tl-03.description: TL-03 — Implement basic ML workflows using Python and standard libraries (scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow).

tl-04.label: LLM Architecture & Training
tl-04.description: TL-04 — Explain LLM architecture and training processes (attention mechanisms, transformers, RLHF, fine-tuning, prompt engineering).

tl-05.label: ML Mathematical Foundations
tl-05.description: TL-05 — Apply mathematical foundations (linear algebra, probability, calculus, optimization) to understand ML system design.

tl-06.label: AI Paradigm Distinctions
tl-06.description: TL-06 — Distinguish among AI paradigms (rule-based systems, narrow ML, LLMs, generative AI, agentic AI) and their capabilities and limits.

tl-07.label: AI System Evaluation
tl-07.description: TL-07 — Evaluate AI system performance, failure modes, and deployment constraints using appropriate frameworks.

cluster-ch.label: Critical & Humanistic Analysis
cluster-ch.description: Cluster CH — Applying humanistic and critical methods to study AI as a historical, cultural, and ideological phenomenon.

ch-01.label: AI Intellectual History
ch-01.description: CH-01 — Situate AI development within its intellectual and political history (from 1940s to present).

ch-02.label: AI Discourse Critique
ch-02.description: CH-02 — Analyze AI discourse, artifacts, and promotional claims as ideologically and rhetorically constructed texts.

ch-03.label: Humanistic Research Methods
ch-03.description: CH-03 — Apply humanistic methods (close reading, ethnography, cultural analysis, oral history) to study AI phenomena.

ch-04.label: Cultural AI Representations
ch-04.description: CH-04 — Critique AI representations in cultural forms (film, theatre, fiction, media) and what they reveal about social anxieties.

ch-05.label: AI Consciousness & Agency
ch-05.description: CH-05 — Evaluate AI claims about consciousness, intelligence, and agency using philosophical and cognitive science frameworks.

ch-06.label: Critical Frameworks for AI
ch-06.description: CH-06 — Analyze AI through feminist, postcolonial, critical race, or disability frameworks to reveal differential impacts.

cluster-ev.label: Ethics & Values
cluster-ev.description: Cluster EV — Applying ethical frameworks and critiquing the limits of ethics discourse in AI governance.

ev-01.label: Ethical Framework Application
ev-01.description: EV-01 — Apply major ethical frameworks (consequentialism, rights-based, virtue ethics, care ethics) to AI deployment decisions.

ev-02.label: Algorithmic Bias
ev-02.description: EV-02 — Identify and analyze sources of algorithmic bias and unfairness across different social domains.

ev-03.label: Responsible AI Instruments
ev-03.description: EV-03 — Evaluate responsible AI instruments (ethics guidelines, model cards, impact assessments, audit frameworks) for their scope and limits.

ev-04.label: High-Stakes Domain Ethics
ev-04.description: EV-04 — Analyze the ethics of AI in high-stakes domains (healthcare, criminal justice, hiring, surveillance, warfare).

ev-05.label: Ethics vs Structural Critique
ev-05.description: EV-05 — Distinguish procedural ethics from structural/systemic critique and recognize when ethics discourse substitutes for governance.

ev-06.label: Privacy & Data Rights
ev-06.description: EV-06 — Assess privacy, consent, and data rights implications of AI systems and their deployment contexts.

cluster-gp.label: Governance & Policy
cluster-gp.description: Cluster GP — Analyzing AI regulatory frameworks, institutional design, and international governance.

gp-01.label: AI Regulatory Frameworks
gp-01.description: GP-01 — Compare AI regulatory frameworks across jurisdictions (EU AI Act, US, UK, China, Global South).

gp-02.label: Governance Institutional Design
gp-02.description: GP-02 — Apply institutional design and political economy analysis to AI governance challenges.

gp-03.label: Historical Policy Analogies
gp-03.description: GP-03 — Draw on historical analogies (nuclear, pharma, aviation, finance) to analyze AI governance options and their limitations.

gp-04.label: AI in the Public Sector
gp-04.description: GP-04 — Evaluate AI in public sector, national security, and government contexts, including autonomous systems and intelligence analysis.

gp-05.label: Technical AI Safety Policy
gp-05.description: GP-05 — Assess technical AI safety proposals (RLHF, red-teaming, evals, constitutional AI) from a policy and governance standpoint.

gp-06.label: International AI Dynamics
gp-06.description: GP-06 — Analyze international AI competition and cooperation, including US-China dynamics and global governance institutions.

cluster-rm.label: Research & Analytical Methods
cluster-rm.description: Cluster RM — Designing and evaluating qualitative, quantitative, and AI-assisted research on AI-related phenomena.

rm-01.label: Qualitative Research Design
rm-01.description: RM-01 — Design and conduct qualitative research (ethnographic fieldwork, elite interviewing, document analysis, discourse analysis).

rm-02.label: Computational Research Design
rm-02.description: RM-02 — Design and conduct computational or quantitative research on AI-related phenomena.

rm-03.label: Evidence Evaluation
rm-03.description: RM-03 — Critically evaluate academic and policy research, distinguishing strong evidence from weak claims.

rm-04.label: Evidence Synthesis
rm-04.description: RM-04 — Synthesize evidence across multiple sources to construct well-supported analytical arguments.

rm-05.label: AI as Research Instrument
rm-05.description: RM-05 — Use AI tools as research instruments (for literature synthesis, data analysis, drafting, and ideation) with critical awareness.

cluster-cw.label: Communication & Writing
cluster-cw.description: Cluster CW — Producing policy, journalistic, and AI-assisted written communication for specialist and general audiences.

cw-01.label: Policy Genre Writing
cw-01.description: CW-01 — Produce policy genre writing (memos, briefs, white papers, testimony) calibrated to non-specialist decision-makers.

cw-02.label: Data-Driven Public Narratives
cw-02.description: CW-02 — Write data-driven narratives for general audiences (journalism, public essays, science writing).

cw-03.label: Technical Concept Translation
cw-03.description: CW-03 — Translate technical AI concepts clearly for lay or policy audiences in written and oral form.

cw-04.label: Persuasive Genre Writing
cw-04.description: CW-04 — Produce persuasive arguments in multiple genres calibrated to specific audiences and institutional contexts.

cw-05.label: AI Writing Tool Use
cw-05.description: CW-05 — Use generative AI tools to enhance and accelerate writing, summarization, and communication workflows.

cw-06.label: AI Writing Quality Direction
cw-06.description: CW-06 — Direct and evaluate AI-assisted writing for policy quality — applying expert judgment to AI-assisted workflows, evaluating output against professional standards, recognizing plausible-but-wrong analysis, and maintaining author accountability.

cluster-hi.label: Human-AI Interaction & Design
cluster-hi.description: Cluster HI — Designing and evaluating AI systems with attention to human oversight, trust, augmentation, and emerging interaction modalities.

hi-01.label: Human-Centered AI Design
hi-01.description: HI-01 — Apply human-centered design principles to evaluate or build AI-enabled systems and interfaces.

hi-02.label: AI Explainability & Trust
hi-02.description: HI-02 — Design for AI explainability, user trust, and appropriate human oversight in AI deployments.

hi-03.label: Augmentation vs Replacement
hi-03.description: HI-03 — Analyze augmentation vs. replacement dynamics in human-AI collaborative systems.

hi-04.label: Human-AI Teaming
hi-04.description: HI-04 — Evaluate human-AI teaming structures, their effects on agency, accountability, and decision quality.

hi-05.label: Emerging AI Modalities
hi-05.description: HI-05 — Assess emerging human-AI modalities (brain-computer interfaces, wearable biometrics, agentic AI) and their social implications.

cluster-sd.label: Societal & Domain Applications
cluster-sd.description: Cluster SD — Evaluating AI's role and impact across healthcare, security, labor, democracy, creative practice, education, and business.

sd-01.label: Healthcare AI
sd-01.description: SD-01 — Evaluate AI applications in healthcare (diagnostics, clinical decision support, equity in access, regulatory approval).

sd-02.label: National Security AI
sd-02.description: SD-02 — Analyze AI in national security contexts (autonomous weapons, intelligence analysis, cyber operations, disinformation).

sd-03.label: Labor & Economic Impact
sd-03.description: SD-03 — Assess AI's impact on labor markets, employment structures, migration, and economic inequality.

sd-04.label: AI & Democracy
sd-04.description: SD-04 — Examine AI's role in democracy, political discourse, media ecosystems, and institutional stability.

sd-05.label: Creative AI Applications
sd-05.description: SD-05 — Apply AI methods and tools in creative domains (music, film, performance, interactive media).

sd-06.label: Educational AI
sd-06.description: SD-06 — Evaluate AI in educational settings (pedagogy design, learning analytics, curriculum development, academic integrity).

sd-07.label: Business AI Applications
sd-07.description: SD-07 — Analyze AI applications in business and marketing contexts (strategy, customer analytics, operations).
